2026-05-03 13:30  ddb9fd9f  [n]    ₢A-AAo    Add 'Enable Password Authentication in sshd_config' step to Windows handbook procedure (between Start/Enable Service and Allow Firewall Port). Empirical finding from rocket bootstrap: Windows OpenSSH 9.5 ships with sshd_config in a state that blocks password auth — the wire protocol advertises keyboard-interactive (the standard wrapper for password delivery on Windows) but the server refuses to actually prompt, producing 'Permission denied (publickey,keyboard-interactive)' with no prompt ever shown to the operator. Microsoft Learn confirms: on Windows OpenSSH the only configurable AuthenticationMethods are 'password' and 'publickey' (KbdInteractiveAuthentication is in the not-available list); the server's PasswordAuthentication directive controls both wire methods. Default-shipped state empirically rejects password auth even when the wire protocol advertises it. New step uses notepad on BUBC_windows_sshd_config (operator already has elevated PowerShell so SYSTEM-owned write succeeds), instructs operator to set PasswordAuthentication yes (or add the line if absent), then Restart-Service sshd. Step prose explains this is temporary state — garrison flips back to 'no' as part of its hardening. Placement is post-Start/Enable (sshd_config doesn't exist until first sshd start) and pre-Firewall (so the server is in the correct state when first reachable from network). Step 3 (Start/Enable) gains a one-line note that first start generates the default sshd_config. Reused BUBC_windows_sshd_config (had been retired from handbook in earlier trim, now re-adopted for this step — single source of truth preserved). Validated via tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh: Windows section now reads 5 numbered steps (Set Password / Install / Start+Enable / Enable PasswordAuth / Firewall) followed by Verify Reachability and Run Garrison sections.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
2026-05-03 12:56  e170740c  [n]    ₢A-AAo    Add explicit 'set or confirm admin password' step to handbook — don't presume operator already knows the local admin password (Windows logon is often via PIN/Hello/Microsoft-account; Linux admin user may have no password set). Windows: new Step 1 (Set or Confirm Admin Password) using `net user <admin-user> <temp-password>`, with note that the value stops affecting SSH after garrison hardens sshd_config; existing 3 steps renumber to 2/3/4 via auto-counter. Dropped redundant 'Admin user account has a known password' precondition bullet (now covered by step). Linux/Mac: parallel guidance added — `sudo passwd <admin-user>` if unsure of the password, with same 'value stops affecting SSH after garrison runs' framing. Operators who prefer pre-establishing key trust can still use ssh-copy-id (kept as alternative). Verified via tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh: Windows section now 4 numbered steps + Verify Reachability + Run Garrison; Linux/Mac section reads cleanly as two paragraphs (manual scope + password setup) plus the optional ssh-copy-id alternative. Landing prose unchanged — 'make sshd reachable on the network with a known admin password' is now backed by explicit guidance for ensuring that password is known. 2026-05-03 12:49  2615224d  [n]    ₢A-AAo    Land dual-purpose-garrison pivot in BUSJGB and trim handbook to manual minimum. BUSJGB amended: dropped 'operator-bootstrapped out-of-band' precondition, replaced with PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password contract (garrison never sees the password — ssh prompts on /dev/tty); reframed NOTE as dual-purpose (front-half steps 1-4 idempotent admin trust + sshd harden, back-half steps 5-9 destructive workload). Two new front-half steps inserted between original step 2 (place admin trust) and step 3 (destroy workload): chmod admin authorized_keys; harden sshd_config (PubkeyAuthentication yes, PasswordAuthentication no, PermitEmptyPasswords no) + validate + restart sshd. Idempotency wording on each front-half step so retries converge. Guarantee + completion clauses extended for sshd hardening. buhj_jurisdiction.sh trimmed: module header rewritten (garrison handles admin trust, not operator); landing renderer flips operator-vs-garrison narrative; Linux/Mac note kept trivial but trimmed of the 'if password disallowed' edge case (no longer relevant under password-fallback); Windows bootstrap collapsed from 9 steps to 3 (Install OpenSSH Server / Start+Enable Service / Allow Firewall Port) + admin-password precondition + Verify Reachability section (its own buh_section to break out of step-3 indent); post-bootstrap renderer mentions first-run password prompt; buc_doc_brief updated; kindle precondition retargeted from BUBC_windows_admin_auth_keys to BUBC_windows_ssh_port (the two retired BUBC vars are no longer rendered in the trim but left in bubc_constants.sh — implement-jurisdiction will need them in the garrison itself). Paddock §Current Concept / §Garrison ceremony / §Standing Notes already matched the new model from prior currying commit d7127ac5; no paddock work needed. Out-of-docket drift spotted but NOT touched: BUSJGC:20 + BUSJGW:25 still carry the dropped 'operator-bootstrapped out-of-band' wording, and BUS0:1107-1109 ('Garrison authenticates using this key only — there is no password fallback. First-time admin trust is established out-of-band...') + BUS0:1462-1463 ('first-time admin SSH bootstrap is an out-of-band handbook procedure') contradict the new model; flagged for a follow-up pace. Verified: tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh renders cleanly (landing → Linux/Mac → Windows 3-step → Verify Reachability → Run Garrison). 2026-05-03 11:49  f3c25bcd  [n]    ₢A-AAo    Land Windows admin SSH bootstrap handbook under jurisdiction. New buhj_jurisdiction.sh module renders BUSJH0 landing + Linux/Mac trivial note + 9-step Windows bootstrap (sshd install, key-only sshd_config hardening, bare admin pubkey placement, icacls lockdown, key-auth verification) + post-bootstrap garrison pointers from a single buw-hj0 tabtarget. Windows OpenSSH layout strings (sshd_config path, administrators_authorized_keys path, port 22, firewall rule + display name) minted in bubc_constants.sh as BUBC_windows_* tinder — single source of truth, shared with future bujb_jurisdiction.sh when implement-jurisdiction lands. buhj_cli.sh thin furnish parallels buhw_cli.sh and burp_cli.sh; sources bubc_constants.sh + buym_yelp + buh_handbook + buhj. buwz_zipper.sh gains a jurisdiction-handbook section enrolling BUWZ_HJ0_TOP. Garrison-destructive model honored throughout: operator places BARE admin pubkey only (NO command= directive) because garrison rewrites authorized_keys with the shell-letter command= on first run; warning prose makes this load-bearing. Content salvaged from buhw_access_base (sshd install + lockdown) + the icacls portion of buhw_access_entrypoints; buhw_access_remote dropped as it conflicted with bus_keys_operator_owned (operator owns key generation; not a handbook concern). buhw_windows.sh and its five buw-HW* tabtargets retained intact for AAn-scope retirement; the new content lives alongside, not on top of, them. buh_index_buk gains a Jurisdiction pointer line. Latent bug surfaced separately during smoke-test (pre-existing at HEAD, not introduced here): BUWZ_* colophon vars are populated by zbuwz_kindle in the workbench process via printf -v which doesn't export, so they're unbound after buz_exec_lookup execs into the CLI process; buw-h0 (via buh_index_buk's BUWZ_HW_TOP ref) and buw-hw (via buhw_top's BUWZ_RHC_LINUX ref) both fail with unbound-variable errors at HEAD before any of these changes. My buh_index_buk addition adds a BUWZ_HJ0_TOP reference of the same shape — same bug, not a new one. buw-hj0 itself renders cleanly because it never traverses buh_index_buk. BCG question handled: Windows path strings are pure literals with no runtime dependency, so they're tinder (lowercase body, no readonly, declared at module top) rather than kindle constants — buhw_windows.sh's existing pattern of declaring them as ZBUHW_* readonly inside kindle is suboptimal but out of scope for retirement-bound code. Operator-on-Windows test still pending (cannot be done from this session); buw-h0 latent-bug resolution still pending decision. Mapping section minted bus_jurisdiction + bujb_jurisdiction + BURC_WORKLOAD_USER + BURP four-field set + 7 new tabtarget quoins; dropped BURW family, old BURP fields, burn_workloads, lieutenancy, and conscript/discharge/inventory verbs. Body: deleted BURW Regime section; BURP body carries ssh-keygen -y derivation note; new Garrison-Destructive Model subsection; 13-tabtarget catalog (6 regime config + 3 per-shell-letter garrison b/c/w + knock + command-file + interactive + handbook; PowerShell deferred); garrison is the sole verb with full ceremony narrative. Subdocs: 7 new BUSJ* minted, 15 retired (11 explicit + 4 BUSTOK/OE/OS/HT whose colophons changed). Grep sweep also corrected BUSTPV/BUSTLV stale field references. All <<anchor,Display>> references and 14 include directives resolve; zero surviving references to dropped concepts. L1 reshaped BURP to four fields (VICEROYALTY/PRIVILEGED_USER/PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE/WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE), added BURC_WORKLOAD_USER as project-wide xname, and minted the bub/bube/bubep_* platform tree with BUBC_platforms_{linux,mac,windows} tinder constants; BURN_PLATFORM enum re-enrolled with bubep_* values; BURN_WORKLOADS dropped. L2 introduced bujb_jurisdiction.sh as the BCG-compliant operational seat housing locked spec data (BUJB_command_{b,c,w}, BUJB_workload_keypath_{b,c,w}, BUJB_wsl_distribution=rbtww-main, BUJB_sshd_hardening) and bujb_cli.sh as the furnish/dispatch wrapper, with bujb_resolve_investiture as the sole single-call-per-process load-and-cross-validate entrypoint (mode 0600 + ssh-keygen -y dry-load on both key files). L3 minted 14 tabtargets (buw-rn[lrv], buw-rp[lrv], buw-jpF, buw-jpG[bcw], buw-jw[kcs], buw-hj0) with correct zipper enrollments and channels; ceremonies implement BUSJPF two-phase fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only, with PowerShell-as-cmd.exe-shell heredoc transport and Restart-Service-disconnect treated as expected phase boundary) and BUSJG{B,C,W} 6-step garrison (admin SSH probe with platform-aware failure pointer, destroy/create workload account per platform, place authorized_keys with shell-letter command= directive, plant workload privkey via base64+heredoc, round-trip validate). L3d cleanup dropped the redundant BUJB_RESOLVED_* publish layer (~55 sites updated to read BURP/BURN/BURC vars directly). L3e relocated 8 pure-literal tinder constants out of zbujb_kindle to module top-level per BCG tinder discipline. L4 swept BUS0 drift (burp_node→burp_viceroyalty, burn_platform Values→bubep_*, sub-letter legend documenting bujb_'s b=bash/p=powershell monosemy), retired the two superseded colophons (buw-HWab AccessBase, buw-HWax AccessEntrypoints) plus their function bodies and zipper enrollments, and rewired the RBK Windows orchestrator (rbhw0_top.sh) to a 6-phase / 11-step setup sequence reflecting the prerequisite chain (SSH reachability → admin trust+harden → environments → workload provisioning → user provisioning → docker). L3f BCG sweep cleared 18 capture/stderr violations in bujb_jurisdiction.sh per BCG line 502/529: zbujb_check_key_file gained a priv|work slot parameter routing stat + ssh-keygen -y dry-load through 8 new ZBUJB_STAT/DRYLOAD_*_{PRIV,WORK} temp-file constants; garrison step4 ssh-keygen pubkey emit + step5 base64 encode + fenestrate phase1 ssh-keygen pubkey emit all moved from $(external) capture to temp-file-then-$(<file) pattern; garrison step5 dirname externals replaced with parameter expansion (z_target_dir=${z_target%/*}); garrison step4/step5 inline $([ z_letter = b ] && echo sudo -n) sudo-prefix dance refactored to precomputed local z_sudo_prefix (eliminating 5 inline $() captures); three internal helpers renamed to _capture form per BCG line 502 exemption (bujb_command_for→bujb_command_for_capture, bujb_workload_keypath_for→bujb_workload_keypath_for_capture, zbujb_workload_home→zbujb_workload_home_capture) with case-default buc_die replaced by return 1 and all 5 caller sites updated to two-line || buc_die discipline. L4 spec command= lock-in inlined the three concrete command= directive strings into BUSJGB/BUSJGC/BUSJGW step 4 as [listing] blocks, byte-for-byte equal to the BUJB_command_{b,c,w} tinder constants, with explicit mirroring notes — making spec the source of truth for the contract content and module the mirror. Verification across the full AAm scope: bash -n green on all edited shell files; tt/buw-st green (5 fixtures, 28 cases unchanged from baseline); tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 25/26 (matches BBAAz baseline, lone failure rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels unchanged); buw-jpF/jpGb/jwk smoke-tests dispatch correctly and die cleanly on missing investiture. Out-of-scope held: PowerShell garrison + dispatch, adopt verb, user regime, Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb, broad cleanup sweep (deferred to AAn), and end-to-end Windows-node test (lives in AAD/E/F). Per-verb platform assertion location (per-verb assert helpers inside public verb function rather than inline at tabtarget) accepted as-is by operator decision; the docket's literal 'inline 2-line check' wording is a minor narrative drift to address at next paddock review. Pre-existing rot in tt/buw-hw (buhw_top references undefined BUWZ_RHC_LINUX/MAC/LOCALHOST + BUWZ_HW_VERIFY_SSH + BUWZ_RN_INSTALL_KEY) confirmed dormant, unchanged by AAm's colophon retirement, deferred to AAn broad sweep. aiTitle: Understanding Docker on Windows and WSL

message.content: How did you discover AAF then if it was dropped?

content.text:
Honest answer: I didn't discover it as an enrolled pace — I read the name "AAF — Docker dual-daemon" from the paddock prose in `gazette_out.md` (the §Heat Sequence section), and presumed it referred to a live placeholder pace.

The enrolled-pace list from `jjx_show` was right there in the same output — six paces, none of them AAF — and I should have noticed the discrepancy. Instead I treated the paddock as authoritative about pace existence and built my recommendation on top of the assumption. That's the failure: paddock prose names a thing, code-of-record says it doesn't exist, and I picked the prose without flagging the gap.

Two things this surfaces:

1. **My methodology was sloppy.** Paddock is the shape of the work; the enrolled-pace list is the truth about what exists. When they disagree, the disagreement is the headline, not a footnote.

2. **The paddock genuinely is stale on those three lines** — that's not made-up by me, the drift is real (paddock cur 2026-05-04 14:42, drops 2026-05-05 12:18). But I didn't notice the drift; I just read past it.

Sorry for the muddied groom output. The structural question I posed (Option A enroll AAF / Option B reslate AAr) still stands, but the framing should have been "AAF was dropped an hour ago; do you want to re-enroll under that name or mint fresh?" rather than "AAF is a placeholder in the paddock."

message.content: Your urge to apologize is disrupting the whole point of my question.  I am trying to fix this for future chats.  Are you saying that pace is mentioned by coronet in the paddock?

content.text:
Yes. The paddock §Heat Sequence has these lines:

```
**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
- AAF — Docker dual-daemon
```

Those are coronet suffixes embedded in paddock prose — referencing specific pace identifiers from inside the document that's supposed to describe the *shape* of the work, not its enrollment ledger.

This is exactly the anti-pattern your JJK context warns about:

> **Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, locked decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Annotating locked decisions with the coronet that landed them ("BBAAM depot-identity-collapse", "BBAAN landed") accumulates into a changelog. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. Don't add such annotations; when editing a paddock for other reasons, prune the ones that aren't load-bearing.

§Heat Sequence here is doing the prohibited thing in two ways at once: keying plan-steps by coronet, *and* preserving them across pace drops. The result is a load-bearing-looking section that's actually rotting silently — paces get dropped/relabeled/transferred and the coronet refs go stale. A future agent reading the paddock cannot tell whether `AAF` is a placeholder, a live pace, a dropped pace, or a typo without cross-checking `jjx_show`.

Fix is structural, not behavioral: prune the coronet-keyed list out of the paddock. The §Heat Sequence content can survive without coronets — "Practice paces: WSL/Cygwin install; JJK fundus inside WSL; Docker dual-daemon" describes shape; the actual enrollment lives in pace state. If a future grooming wants to know what's left, `jjx_show` answers that authoritatively.

Same surgery probably worth applying to the §Implementation and §Investigation lines just above (`AAe` is also coronet-named in prose).

message.content: Before that pruning, I need your advice on whether we need to strengthen CLAUDE.md/ jjk*.md wording to prevent coronets from being written into paddock in the first place?  I'm pretty sure that never helps.

content.text:
Yes — the existing wording is too narrow. It catches the retrospective shape of the failure but not the prospective one, which is what bit us here.

What the current rule actually prohibits:

> Annotating locked decisions with the coronet that landed them ("BBAAM depot-identity-collapse", "BBAAN landed")

That's retrospective tagging — coronets pinned to *closed* work. Both examples are past-tense.

What the paddock here actually did:

```
- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
- AAF — Docker dual-daemon
```

That's prospective — coronets pinned to *planned* work. An agent reading the current rule would reasonably conclude "I'm not annotating a landed thing, I'm sketching the plan ahead — different case." The rule's letter doesn't reach this shape, even though the rot vector is identical.

The unifying principle the existing wording hints at but doesn't state: **a coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data.** The paddock describes shape; `jjx_show` describes enrollment. Any coronet in paddock prose — past, present, or future — creates stale-by-mount risk, because every pace-state operation (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer, re-enroll) can invalidate the ref while the paddock prose silently keeps it. Even if the pace stays alive, its coronet drifts in meaning relative to the prose around it.

Suggested strengthening: replace the retrospective-only framing with the principle. Something like — and treat as sketch, not draft —

> Paddocks describe shape; coronets identify paces in the enrollment ledger. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work), not prospectively (naming planned paces), not as cross-references. If the paddock needs to point at work, name the work by its purpose, not its identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.

Two reasons to make it absolute rather than guidance-with-exceptions:

1. The "useful coronet ref" case is hard to construct. Even cross-heat dependencies can be expressed by purpose ("blocks on the BURP-collapse work in the prior heat") without a coronet.
2. An absolute rule is greppable. `grep -E '₢|\bA[A-Z][A-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]\b' <paddock>` becomes a lint check; a guidance-with-exceptions rule does not.

Worth also extending to firemarks in paddock prose for the same reason (heat-internal paddocks shouldn't need to name their own firemark; cross-heat refs by purpose, not identity).

message.content: Lets just focus on coronets for now, I'll permit references to firemarks because they change relatively infrequently.  State a precise jjk*.md repair inspired by your draft above for my review.

content.text:
Proposed edit to `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/jjk-claude-context.md`. This replaces the existing Paddock-posture paragraph (currently in the JJK Concepts block, just after the Spook bullet).

**Old:**

> **Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, locked decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Annotating locked decisions with the coronet that landed them ("BBAAM depot-identity-collapse", "BBAAN landed") accumulates into a changelog. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. Don't add such annotations; when editing a paddock for other reasons, prune the ones that aren't load-bearing.

**New:**

> **Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, locked decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.

Three things this changes vs. current:

1. **Promotes the rule from "don't annotate" to "coronets do not appear."** Absolute, covers both retrospective and prospective shapes.
2. **Names the principle** — coronet = enrollment-ledger key, not shape data — so future readers can extend the rule to cases the examples don't cover.
3. **Explicitly permits firemarks** with a one-clause rationale, so the asymmetry is intentional rather than an oversight.

Two things deliberately preserved from current:

- The "git log + `jjx_log` are the journal" framing.
- The "when editing a paddock for other reasons, prune them" closing clause — keeps the cleanup obligation when an agent encounters legacy violations.

Does it require a corresponding edit to `JJS0_JobJockeySpec.adoc`? Probably worth grepping to see if the spec carries paddock-posture wording too — but I haven't checked, and you may want this CLAUDE-context update to land standalone first.

Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.

lines: **Reference discipline.** Pace order is the dependency tree — single-operator workflow runs paces in heat order, so explicit dependency markers in docket prose are usually overspecified. Coronet cross-refs in dockets earn their keep only when the dependency crosses heats or skips order — rare, not never.

-**Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, locked decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Annotating locked decisions with the coronet that landed them ("BBAAM depot-identity-collapse", "BBAAN landed") accumulates into a changelog. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. Don't add such annotations; when editing a paddock for other reasons, prune the ones that aren't load-bearing.

+**Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, locked decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.

**Mid-execution posture.** When a failure or surprise surfaces while a pace is mounted, the default response is mechanism + one specific repair you'd attempt. Do not proliferate options or weigh consequences across alternatives — pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. If the repair is obvious, proceed; if not, surface the question and stop.

content.content:
# JJK Kit Source

Edits here apply to the kit source only. Do not modify the active installation in `.claude/` or the parent project's CLAUDE.md.

820	A jockey wearing silks, a horse being schooled in a dressage ring, a volte circling back to the same point with refined intent — these images persist across sessions in ways that "human-claimed-pending-interactive-work" cannot.
821	The cognitive cost of re-establishing context drops when the terms are vivid and the metaphor is coherent.
822	
823	=== Racing Vocabulary
824	
825	The original layer, established in V3.
826	Terms drawn from horse racing — the competitive, forward-driving aspect of initiative management.
827	
828	* {jjdhr_heat} — a bounded initiative. A race: defined start, defined finish, sustained effort.
829	* {jjdpr_pace} — a discrete action within a heat. The rhythm of hooves — each stride advances the race.
830	* {jjdt_firemark} — heat identity, `₣`-prefixed. The hot-iron brand that marks ownership on a living animal — permanent, unmistakable.
831	* {jjdt_coronet} — pace identity, `₢`-prefixed. The marking at the hoof line — fine-grained, below the firemark, identifying the individual step.
832	* {jjdt_silks} — display name. The jockey's colors — vivid, human-recognizable, worn for the crowd, not the horse.
833	* {jjdgr_gallops} — the persistent data store. Training grounds where real work happens before race day — not the race itself, but where readiness is built.
834	* *paddock* — heat-wide context. The gathering area where horses are assessed before entering the course.
835	* *muster* — list heats. Assembling the field before the race.
836	* *parade* — show heat or pace detail. The formal presentation of horses before the crowd.
837	* *furlough* — alter heat status. A horse temporarily withdrawn from racing — not retired, not scratched, resting.
838	* *retire* — archive a completed heat. The horse leaves the racing life — honored, not discarded.
839	* *rail* — reorder paces. The rail governs position on the track — who runs where.
840	
841	=== Dressage Vocabulary
842	
843	The V4 layer, introduced for the school-breeze-corral execution pipeline.
844	Terms drawn from dressage and classical horsemanship — the disciplined, precise, iterative aspect of training and evaluation.
845	
846	* *school* — per-pace planning phase. Schooling a horse: patient, attentive work that develops capability before asking for performance. The rider (human) is fully present.
847	* *longe* — heat-level readiness assessment. Working a horse on a long line to assess soundness before mounting — observation without commitment.
848	* *breeze* — autonomous execution phase. A breeze work: the horse runs at controlled speed, demonstrating trained capability without the rider directing each stride.
849	* *corral* — review and integration phase. Gathering horses after work — assessing condition, accepting or turning back for more training.
850	* *volte* — an execution attempt covering one or more paces. A dressage figure: a precise, controlled circle back to the same point with refined intent. Each volte attempts the same work with accumulated understanding.
851	* *caracole* — volte identity, `₡`-prefixed. A cavalry half-turn — circling back for another pass at the same objective.
852	* *gait* — reusable recipe of beats. The trained movement patterns a horse carries — walk, trot, canter, each with distinct rhythm and purpose.
853	* *beat* — atomic execution unit. The indivisible footfall within a gait — one hoof striking ground.
854	* *quirt* — gait identity, `Ꝗ`-prefixed. A short riding whip — the thing that sets a gait in motion.
855	* *reined* — under human authority. The rider holds the reins; the pipeline does not touch this horse.
856	
857	=== Presentation Aliases
858	
859	The display and output layer uses distinct terms for certain schema fields, following the same vocabulary isolation principle:

A jockey wearing silks, a horse being schooled in a dressage ring, a volte circling back to the same point with refined intent — these images persist across sessions in ways that "human-claimed-pending-interactive-work" cannot.
The cognitive cost of re-establishing context drops when the terms are vivid and the metaphor is coherent.

=== Racing Vocabulary

The original layer, established in V3.
Terms drawn from horse racing — the competitive, forward-driving aspect of initiative management.

* {jjdhr_heat} — a bounded initiative. A race: defined start, defined finish, sustained effort.
* {jjdpr_pace} — a discrete action within a heat. The rhythm of hooves — each stride advances the race.
* {jjdt_firemark} — heat identity, `₣`-prefixed. The hot-iron brand that marks ownership on a living animal — permanent, unmistakable.
* {jjdt_coronet} — pace identity, `₢`-prefixed. The marking at the hoof line — fine-grained, below the firemark, identifying the individual step.
* {jjdt_silks} — display name. The jockey's colors — vivid, human-recognizable, worn for the crowd, not the horse.
* {jjdgr_gallops} — the persistent data store. Training grounds where real work happens before race day — not the race itself, but where readiness is built.
* *paddock* — heat-wide context. The gathering area where horses are assessed before entering the course.
* *muster* — list heats. Assembling the field before the race.
* *parade* — show heat or pace detail. The formal presentation of horses before the crowd.
* *furlough* — alter heat status. A horse temporarily withdrawn from racing — not retired, not scratched, resting.
* *retire* — archive a completed heat. The horse leaves the racing life — honored, not discarded.
* *rail* — reorder paces. The rail governs position on the track — who runs where.

=== Dressage Vocabulary

The V4 layer, introduced for the school-breeze-corral execution pipeline.
Terms drawn from dressage and classical horsemanship — the disciplined, precise, iterative aspect of training and evaluation.

* *school* — per-pace planning phase. Schooling a horse: patient, attentive work that develops capability before asking for performance. The rider (human) is fully present.
* *longe* — heat-level readiness assessment. Working a horse on a long line to assess soundness before mounting — observation without commitment.
* *breeze* — autonomous execution phase. A breeze work: the horse runs at controlled speed, demonstrating trained capability without the rider directing each stride.
* *corral* — review and integration phase. Gathering horses after work — assessing condition, accepting or turning back for more training.
* *volte* — an execution attempt covering one or more paces. A dressage figure: a precise, controlled circle back to the same point with refined intent. Each volte attempts the same work with accumulated understanding.
* *caracole* — volte identity, `₡`-prefixed. A cavalry half-turn — circling back for another pass at the same objective.
* *gait* — reusable recipe of beats. The trained movement patterns a horse carries — walk, trot, canter, each with distinct rhythm and purpose.
* *beat* — atomic execution unit. The indivisible footfall within a gait — one hoof striking ground.
* *quirt* — gait identity, `Ꝗ`-prefixed. A short riding whip — the thing that sets a gait in motion.
* *reined* — under human authority. The rider holds the reins; the pipeline does not touch this horse.

=== Presentation Aliases

The display and output layer uses distinct terms for certain schema fields, following the same vocabulary isolation principle:

content.text:
Survey result: **no corresponding update needed in JJS0.**

JJS0's paddock references are all structural, not posture:

- **Line 834** — vocabulary entry. Defines "paddock" metaphorically: *"heat-wide context. The gathering area where horses are assessed before entering the course."* No content-discipline language.
- **Lines 1366–1384** — `jjdhm_paddock` member definition. Specifies the path-to-file field and its filename encoding scheme. No prose-shape rules.
- **Line 2835** — operations table entry: "Recompute `paddock_file` from firemark for every heat." Mechanical only.
- **Line 330** — unrelated; concerns gallops JSON serialization (`git can serve as the journalling layer`).

Searching the whole spec for `shape`, `journal`, `articulat`, `locked`, `posture` returns one hit (line 330, gallops/git context) — none of the paddock-posture vocabulary you established in CLAUDE-context appears in the spec.

**Read:** the layering is intentional and clean. JJS0 specifies the data structure and operations (what a paddock *is* and how the system manipulates it). The content-posture rules — what *belongs* in paddock prose vs. what's journal-rot — live in `jjk-claude-context.md` as agent guidance. The two layers don't overlap on this concern, so the strengthened coronet rule doesn't need a JJS0 mirror.

If you ever wanted JJS0 to carry a content-posture statement, it would slot in around line 1366 (the `jjdhm_paddock` definition) — but I'd argue against it. JJS0 is the spec for code, not for human/agent authoring discipline; mixing them dilutes both.

content.content: M Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/jjk-claude-context.md

params.intent: Strengthen paddock-posture rule to forbid coronets in paddock prose. The prior wording prohibited only retrospective coronet annotations ("BBAAM landed"-style); a real failure surfaced where the AAF/AAE/AAD coronets were embedded prospectively in the §Heat Sequence section and silently rotted when AAF was dropped. Replacement promotes the rule from "don't annotate" to absolute "coronets do not appear in paddock prose" — covers retrospective, prospective, and cross-reference shapes — and names the principle (coronet = enrollment-ledger key, not shape data) so future readers can extend the rule beyond the examples. Firemarks remain explicitly permitted (heats change rarely). JJS0 surveyed and intentionally not modified: the spec carries paddock structure/operations only, not content-posture; layering stays clean.
